context: Your glory is a reference to the shekinah, or the manifest glory of God to Israel, who dwells in the tabernacle and the temple, showing His presence among the people. For Christians, the manifest glory of God to Israel is none other than our Lord Jesus Christ, who before His Incarnation made Himself present among the Israelites ([[EXO.24:16]], [[EXO.24:17]]; 40:34, 35; Lv 9:23; [[2CH.5:14]]; 7:1-3).

Chose this city: Jerusalem is specifically mentioned throughout the Old Testament as having been the city God has chosen for His name (see 3Kg 8:44, 48; 11:32, 36; 14:21; [[2KI.23:27]]; [[2CH.6:34]], [[2CH.6:38]]; 12:13; WSir 49:6).

Chose this city: Jerusalem is specifically mentioned throughout the Old Testament as having been the city God has chosen for His name (see 3Kg 8:44, 48; 11:32, 36; 14:21; [[2KI.23:27]]; [[2CH.6:34]], [[2CH.6:38]]; 12:13; WSir 49:6).

Eleazar likens Israel to that portion of the sacrifices which was set aside for Aaron and his sons (the priests) as their perpetual due from Israel (Lv 7:34, 35; [[NUM.18:8]]-18). The Jews have become the inheritance of the Lord ([[PSA.27:9]]; 32:12; Is 19:25). More than a chosen nation, they have been set apart for holiness. Sennacherib (v. 5) was the king of Assyria during the reign of Hezekiah, king of Judah. The episode noted is the occasion when, at the supplication of Israel, God sent an angel who in one night slew 185,000 of Sennacherib's forces, sending him home to Nineveh in humiliation and defeat. He was assassinated shortly afterwards ([[2KI.18:13]]; 19:35-37; WSir 48:18-21).

Chose this city: Jerusalem is specifically mentioned throughout the Old Testament as having been the city God has chosen for His name (see 3Kg 8:44, 48; 11:32, 36; 14:21; [[2KI.23:27]]; [[2CH.6:34]], [[2CH.6:38]]; 12:13; WSir 49:6).

God the most High: Another ancient way of referring to the God of Israel, and one frequently used in localities where non-Jews abound (see [[GEN.14:19]]-22; [[DAN.3:26]]; [[MRK.5:7]]; [[LUK.8:28]]; [[ACT.16:17]]).

Ode of their fathers: Undoubtedly a psalm or the song of the three young men in the fiery furnace ([[DAN.3:23]]-91). This would have become a regular feature in Jewish liturgical practice. How much more appropriate at such a celebration--and everyone would know the words!

Ode of their fathers: Undoubtedly a psalm or the song of the three young men in the fiery furnace ([[DAN.3:23]]-91). This would have become a regular feature in Jewish liturgical practice. How much more appropriate at such a celebration--and everyone would know the words!

References to God as King of the heavens include [[DAN.4:37]]; 1Ez 4:46, 58; [[JDT.9:12]]; Tb 13:7, 11.

Your glory is a reference to the shekinah, or the manifest glory of God to Israel, who dwells in the tabernacle and the temple, showing His presence among the people. For Christians, the manifest glory of God to Israel is none other than our Lord Jesus Christ, who before His Incarnation made Himself present among the Israelites ([[EXO.24:16]], [[EXO.24:17]]; 40:34, 35; Lv 9:23; [[2CH.5:14]]; 7:1-3).

Your glory is a reference to the shekinah, or the manifest glory of God to Israel, who dwells in the tabernacle and the temple, showing His presence among the people. For Christians, the manifest glory of God to Israel is none other than our Lord Jesus Christ, who before His Incarnation made Himself present among the Israelites ([[EXO.24:16]], [[EXO.24:17]]; 40:34, 35; Lv 9:23; [[2CH.5:14]]; 7:1-3).

Evidently, Ptolemy thought the Jews were making up the law to keep him out. They read him the law to try to convince him ([[EXO.30:10]]; Lv 16:2, 11, 12, 15, 34). The king feels slighted and sees the high priest as beneath him.

God the most High: Another ancient way of referring to the God of Israel, and one frequently used in localities where non-Jews abound (see [[GEN.14:19]]-22; [[DAN.3:26]]; [[MRK.5:7]]; [[LUK.8:28]]; [[ACT.16:17]]).

References to God as King of the heavens include [[DAN.4:37]]; 1Ez 4:46, 58; [[JDT.9:12]]; Tb 13:7, 11.
While living under Gentile dominion, the Jews had to ask permission of the king to enforce their own laws with regard to putting someone to death (see [[JOH.18:31]]).

Such an unusual command can only demonstrate Ptolemy's irrational rage at the Jews. Recall that at the time of the Nativity of Christ, the Jews were called to be registered and enrolled, each with his own tribe ([[LUK.2:1]]-4).

Such an unusual command can only demonstrate Ptolemy's irrational rage at the Jews. Recall that at the time of the Nativity of Christ, the Jews were called to be registered and enrolled, each with his own tribe ([[LUK.2:1]]-4).

Such an unusual command can only demonstrate Ptolemy's irrational rage at the Jews. Recall that at the time of the Nativity of Christ, the Jews were called to be registered and enrolled, each with his own tribe ([[LUK.2:1]]-4).

God the most High: Another ancient way of referring to the God of Israel, and one frequently used in localities where non-Jews abound (see [[GEN.14:19]]-22; [[DAN.3:26]]; [[MRK.5:7]]; [[LUK.8:28]]; [[ACT.16:17]]).

The Jews have bade each other farewell (v. 49), but still call on the Lord for help even against all odds and cry out for mercy. In the Gospels, there are seven instances of someone calling on Jesus for mercy ([[MAT.9:27]]; 15:22; 17:15; 20:30, 31; [[MRK.10:47]], [[MRK.10:48]]; Lk 17:13; 18:38, 39). In every case, they receive it.

In every case, they receive it.

God the most High: Another ancient way of referring to the God of Israel, and one frequently used in localities where non-Jews abound (see [[GEN.14:19]]-22; [[DAN.3:26]]; [[MRK.5:7]]; [[LUK.8:28]]; [[ACT.16:17]]).

Eleazar likens Israel to that portion of the sacrifices which was set aside for Aaron and his sons (the priests) as their perpetual due from Israel (Lv 7:34, 35; [[NUM.18:8]]-18). The Jews have become the inheritance of the Lord ([[PSA.27:9]]; 32:12; Is 19:25). More than a chosen nation, they have been set apart for holiness. Sennacherib (v. 5) was the king of Assyria during the reign of Hezekiah, king of Judah. The episode noted is the occasion when, at the supplication of Israel, God sent an angel who in one night slew 185,000 of Sennacherib's forces, sending him home to Nineveh in humiliation and defeat. He was assassinated shortly afterwards ([[2KI.18:13]]; 19:35-37; WSir 48:18-21).

Eleazar likens Israel to that portion of the sacrifices which was set aside for Aaron and his sons (the priests) as their perpetual due from Israel (Lv 7:34, 35; [[NUM.18:8]]-18). The Jews have become the inheritance of the Lord ([[PSA.27:9]]; 32:12; Is 19:25). More than a chosen nation, they have been set apart for holiness. Sennacherib (v. 5) was the king of Assyria during the reign of Hezekiah, king of Judah. The episode noted is the occasion when, at the supplication of Israel, God sent an angel who in one night slew 185,000 of Sennacherib's forces, sending him home to Nineveh in humiliation and defeat. He was assassinated shortly afterwards ([[2KI.18:13]]; 19:35-37; WSir 48:18-21).

References to God as Father exist several times in the Old Testament (2:21; [[PSA.88:26]]; Tb 13:4; WSol 2:16; WSir 23:4; 2Ez 1:29).

Those registered are those who willingly comply. They will be branded, and this mark of allegiance will be permanent. Some people in ancient times would mark or brand themselves with the symbol of their god, here the ivy leaf. St. John predicts a similar mark of the beast ([[REV.13:16]], [[REV.13:17]]) in the end times.

Punishment on... Pharaoh: See Ex 5-12; [[ROM.9:17]].
